revision.text:
[[ja:伊須流岐比古神社]] {{infobox religious building | name=伊須流岐比古神社 | image=File:Isurugihiko-jinja shrine.jpg | address=[[Ishikawa Prefecture|石川県]][[Kashima District, Ishikawa|鹿島郡]][[Nakanoto, Ishikawa|中能登町]]石動山子部1番地1 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|36|57|57|N|136|58|16|E|scale:25000}} | deity={{ill|Q11585202|qid=Q11585202|1=Isurugi Gongen}} | 創建=（伝）{{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|qid=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō}}元年（[[717]]） | 社格={{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}（小）
{{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|qid=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}}二宮
旧{{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|qid=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|qid=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} | 本殿={{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} | festival={{ill|July 7|en|July 7|qid=Q2694|lt=7月7日|1=July 7}} | 神事= | prefecture={{ill|Nakanoto|qid=Q1349436|1=Nakanoto, Ishikawa}} | coordinates={{Coord|36.96583|N|136.97111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|36.9657|136.97dd}}|caption=Isurugihiko Shrine|width=256}} '''Isurugihiko Shrine''' (伊須流岐比古神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at the summit of {{ill|Mount Sekidō|en|Mount Sekidō|qid=Q11585200|lt=Mount Isurugi|1=Mount Sekidō}} (Ōmimae) in {{ill|Nakanoto, Ishikawa|en|Nakanoto, Ishikawa|qid=Q1349436|lt=Nakanoto, Ishikawa|1=Nakanoto, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Kashima District, Ishikawa|en|Kashima District, Ishikawa|qid=Q1130967|lt=Kashima District, Ishikawa|1=Kashima District, Ishikawa}}, {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}}. It is also written as '''Isurugihiko Shrine''' (伊須留岐比古神社) and is the second-Ranked shrine (二宮) of {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|qid=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines {{ill|Isurugihiko-no-Kami|ja|伊須流岐比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugihiko-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579264|1=Q135579264}} (also known as Ishurugi-hiko) and {{ill|Kikurihime|lt=Kikurihime|lt=Hakusan-Hime-no-Kami|ja|白山比咩神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan-Hime-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 菊理媛神}}. Among them, the main deity, Isurugihiko-no-Kami, appears in the ''Kōkō Sentatsu Roku'' (肯構泉達録) and other sources as a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|qid=Q178885|lt=kami|1=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. He is also identified with {{ill|Isurugi Gongen|ja|石動権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Gongen|qq=}}, one of the {{ill|Gosha Gongen|ja|五社権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gosha Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579265|1=Q135579265}}. The ancient name of Mount Isurugi, "Isurugi" or "Yusurugi," means "a mountain where stones move," which is thought to originate from Daoist star worship and influences from {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}}, as well as the legend of a divine stone that descended from the heavens. This stone was said to tremble and manifest divine power. The shrine was founded to pacify the trembling stone and enshrine it as a deity. During the Meiji period's {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}}, the name "Gongen" was rejected, and the deity is now called Isurugihiko-no-Kami or Ishurugi-hiko. {{ill|Kikurihime|lt=Kikurihime|lt=Hakusan-Hime-no-Kami|ja|白山比咩神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakusan-Hime-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 菊理媛神}} is also enshrined as an {{ill|Aiden-shin|ja|相殿|1=Saijin|lt=Aiden-shin|fr|Saijin|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aiden-shin|13=User:Immanuelle/Saijin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祭神}} deity. Additionally, Ishurugi-hiko and Hakusan-Hime-no-Kami have been associated with {{ill|Izanagi|en|Izanagi|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi|1=Izanagi}} and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}. The shrine's historical Buddhist counterpart deities (本地仏) were {{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō Bosatsu|1=Ākāśagarbha}} (for Isurugihiko-no-Kami) and {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} (for Hakusan-Hime-no-Kami). == History == Since ancient times, a shrine has existed on Mount Isurugi (Ōmimae), worshipped by fishermen and farmers. This shrine is identified with the ''Isurugihiko Shrine'' mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}'s list of shrines. It is one of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} recorded in the ''Engishiki''. According to the ''Isurugi-san Koyengi'' (石動山古縁起), the mountain was opened by the hermit {{ill|Hōdō Sennin|ja|方道仙人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōdō Sennin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the 6th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}, and in 717, the monk Chitoku Shōnin ascended the mountain and built the Great Ritual Hall (講堂) in 756 (''Tenpyō Shōhō'' 8).{{Cite journal|和書|author=小林忠雄 |date=1996-03 |url=https://doi.org/10.15024/00000767 |title=都市空間の原初形態 : 山岳寺院の構造と広場性 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |ISSN=0286-7400 |publisher=国立歴史民俗博物館 |volume=67 |pages=95-136 |doi=10.15024/00000767 |naid=120005748043}} Alternatively, a long-standing tradition attributes the shrine's founding to {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|qid=Q2388439|lt=Taichō|1=Taichō}}, and the ''Shin Engi'' written by {{ill|Hayashi Razan|en|Hayashi Razan|qid=Q558279|lt=Hayashi Razan|1=Hayashi Razan}} in the early modern period also attributes its establishment to Taichō. In the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, Shingon monks constructed temple buildings, leading to the shrine being called "Isurugi Temple" (石動寺) under the {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} system. It became a major religious center, boasting over 360 temple lodges and around 3,000 monks. However, according to the ''Taiheiki'', during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, the temple supported the Southern Court and was burned down by enemy forces (First Battle of Mount Isurugi). It was later rebuilt under the patronage of the Ashikaga shogunate and became a subsidiary of {{ill|Kajū-ji|en|Kajū-ji|qid=Q772598|lt=Kanjū-ji|1=Kajū-ji}}, adopting the name ''Tenpyō-ji''. In 1582, the shrine was again burned down when the monks sided with Noto loyalists against {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}}'s forces (Second Battle of Mount Isurugi, {{ill|Arayama Battle|ja|荒山合戦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arayama Battle|1=Arayama Battle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28683115}}). The current main shrine was built in 1653 by {{ill|Maeda Toshitsune|en|Maeda Toshitsune|qid=Q587204|lt=Maeda Toshitsune|1=Maeda Toshitsune}} of the {{ill|Kaga Domain|en|Kaga Domain|qid=Q903448|lt=Kaga Domain|1=Kaga Domain}} and was originally called "Ōmiya." == See also == * {{ill|Mount Sekidō|en|Mount Sekidō|qid=Q11585200|lt=Mount Isurugi|1=Mount Sekidō}} * {{ill|Isurugi Gongen|ja|石動権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Gongen|qq=}} * {{ill|Isurugi Shrine|ja|石動神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isurugi Shrine|1=Isurugi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11585204}} * {{ill|Ishikawa Prefecture|en|Ishikawa Prefecture|qid=Q131281|lt=Ishikawa Prefecture|1=Ishikawa Prefecture}} ) * {{ill|Tōnomine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōnomine|qq=}} Myōraku-ji (now {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanzan Shrine|qq=}} ) * {{ill|Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyamadera|lt=Ōyama-dera|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyama-dera|qq=}} * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa-taisha|qq=}} * {{ill|Itsukushima Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itsukushima Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Munakata Taisha|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Taisha|qq=}} * {{ill|Usa Jingū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Jingū|qq=}} * {{ill|Aso Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aso Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Hiko-san|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiko-san|qq=}} Reisō-ji == References == == Bibliography == * {{ill|Kuroda Toshio|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroda Toshio|qq=}} . *Temple and Shrine Powers: Another Medieval Society.* Iwanami Shinsho, 1980. * {{ill|Itō Masatoshi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Itō Masatoshi|qq=}} . *The Medieval Religious Powers: Migration and Connection.* Chikuma Shinsho, 2008. == See Also == * {{ill|Kenmitsu System|ja|顕密体制|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmitsu System|qq=}} * {{ill|Kenmon System Theory|ja|権門体制|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenmon System Theory|qq=}} * {{ill|Right of asylum|en|Right of asylum|qid=Q11283415|lt=Right of asylum|1=Right of asylum}} * {{ill|Temple Precinct Cities|ja|境内都市|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temple Precinct Cities|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinaimachi|fr|Jinaicho|ja|寺内町|lt=Teramachi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinaimachi|qq=}} * {{ill|Sōhei|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sōhei|qq=}} * {{ill|Jinin|ja|神人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinin|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|伊須流岐比古神社|version=99376454|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Isurugihiko-jinja shrine.jpg|280px]]
